\section{Evaluation}
\label{sec:eval}

We evaluate two things separately, because they have different epistemic status.
The \emph{security} property---omission detection under a Byzantine
coordinator---is deterministic protocol logic and does not depend on Fabric or
hardware, so we measure it exactly with a reference simulator that runs from a
clean checkout. The \emph{performance} cost depends on the Fabric deployment and
host, so those numbers come from the testbed; where they have not yet been
recorded they are marked \texttt{TO\_BE\_MEASURED} rather than estimated.

\subsection{Omission detection (deterministic, reproducible)}

We instantiate one source channel producing eight contiguous windows under a
$2$-of-$3$ endorsement policy, with a freshness deadline of $\Delta=3$
coordination blocks. A Byzantine coordinator controlling a sub-quorum of source
peers attempts the six behaviours of Table~\ref{tab:adv}; an honest baseline is
included as a control. The outcomes are produced by
\texttt{run\_security\_battery.py} and are reproducible with \texttt{make
security}.

\input{paper/tab_security.tex}

Every omission attempt is detected, and the detecting mechanism matches the
theory. Content tampering, reordering, and fabrication are caught at commit time
because the modified root no longer carries a valid source endorsement
(Thm.~\ref{thm:within}--\ref{thm:reorder}); interior-window dropping and replay
are caught at commit time by the monotonicity/contiguity guard
(Thm.~\ref{thm:interior},~\ref{thm:replay}); tail truncation, which is the
\emph{absence} of an anchor and therefore invisible to commit-time checks, is
caught by the verifier's freshness deadline. The honest baseline is accepted,
confirming the verifier does not reject indiscriminately. Crucially, the
verifier reads only the coordination channel: it never accesses source-channel
contents, consistent with the confidentiality model and the necessity argument
of Proposition~\ref{prop:necessity}.

\subsection{Cost}

Table~\ref{tab:cost} reports per-operation cost. Asymptotic complexity and the
per-anchor world-state size are real and deterministic. The world-state cost is
$O(1)$ per anchor---one Merkle root plus the endorsement, not the leaves---so it
is independent of the number of events per window; for a $2$-of-$3$ policy a
persisted anchor record is a few hundred bytes regardless of window size. The
verifier stores nothing beyond what it reads.

\input{paper/tab_cost.tex}

The Fabric per-operation latency rows are \texttt{TO\_BE\_MEASURED}: they require
the single-host testbed (\texttt{network/README.md}) and are filled by
\texttt{bench/bench\_fabric.js} (median and p95 over $100$ iterations after
$10$ warmup), after which \texttt{make tables} regenerates this table. As an
implementation-level sanity check on asymptotics only---\emph{not} a Fabric
measurement---a local micro-benchmark of the commitment primitive
(\texttt{bench/local\_crypto\_bench.js}) confirms the expected $O(N)$ growth of
root construction in the number of events per window; those host-local numbers
are reported in the artifact and are deliberately excluded from
Table~\ref{tab:cost}.

\subsection{Threats to validity and scope}

The security results are only as strong as the modelled adversary: we model a
Byzantine coordinator plus a sub-quorum of source peers, and we assume the
honest source quorum and the coordination channel's ordering service behave
correctly (the latter is the standard Fabric trust assumption). The protocol
detects omission of cross-domain-relevant events as defined by the source-side
relevance predicate $\rho_s$; it does not police whether $\rho_s$ itself is
correctly specified, nor does it enforce the downstream \emph{effects} of an
omitted event---out of scope by design. Finally, the prototype's endorsement
verification uses a keyed-hash stand-in for source-MSP signatures; the security
argument is unchanged under ECDSA/MSP, but a full multi-MSP deployment is needed
before quoting production latency.
